coredata.dc:title: The performance of metal carbon-based quantum dots as an anti-bacterial factor in green conditions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ansari, Payam
Ghobadifard, Mahdieh
Mohebbi, Sajjad
Ashengroph, Morahem
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots and metal-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs and M-CQDs) were prepared by the hydrothermal method. The samples were characterized by EDS, UV–Vis, FT-IR, XRD, and PL techniques. FT-IR investigation demonstrates that M-CQDs have a shift of the peak associated with C-O and C=O oxygen bonds due to doping metals into carbon quantum dots, and a band at 550 cm-1, which is related to M-O bonds. N-CQDs and M-CQDs exhibited impressive PL behavior as an antibacterial factor against representative Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacterial strains. According to the findings and comparisons of the average halo zone of the anti-bactericidal activity of the prepared samples and MIC methods, N-CQDs and M-CQDs demonstrate excellent prevention versus bacteria with the best MIC of 0.5 mg/L. Co-CQDs had the greatest inhibitory effect against S. aureus with an average halo zone diameter of 15 mm, and E. coli with an average halo zone diameter of 13 mm. This toxicity is due to the generation of reactive oxygen species and the interaction between the M-CQDs and cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anti-bacterial activity
Metal doping
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Antimicrobial properties of synthetic quantum dots on the tested pathogenic bacteria

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors like to acknowledge the University of Kurdistan, Ayatollah Boroujerdi University, and the Labs Nets for their support in the characterization of materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis curves and (b) PL spectra of N-CQDs, Co-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, Fe-CQDs, Ni-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs.
Deconvolution of PL spectra of (a) N-CQDs, (b) Co-CQDs, (c) Cu-CQDs, (d) Fe-CQDs, (e) Ni-CQDs, and (f) Zn-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs, Co-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, Fe-CQDs, Ni-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs and (b) XRD pattern of N-CQDs and M-CQDs.
(a) TEM and (b and c) HRTEM images of M-CQDs.
EDS of N-CQDs, Co-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, Fe-CQDs, Ni-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs.
Raman spectrum of N-CQDs and M-CQDS.
Antibacterial activity of M-CQDs against E. coli and S. aureus. Statistical analysis confirms Co-CQDs have significantly higher antibacterial activity (p < 0.0001).

Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of various carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) formulations against E. coli and S. aureus. The color changes in the wells indicate bacterial inhibition, comparing the antibacterial activity of CQD formulations with chloramphenicol as a broad-spectrum antibiotic.
Diagram of the synthesis of N-CQDs, and M-CQDs.

head.ce:title: The performance of metal carbon-based quantum dots as an anti-bacterial factor in green conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, P.O. Box 416, Sanandaj, Kurdistan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dResearch Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, P.O. Box 416, Sanandaj, Kurdistan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots and metal-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs and M-CQDs) were prepared by the hydrothermal method. The samples were characterized by EDS, UV–Vis, FT-IR, XRD, and PL techniques. FT-IR investigation demonstrates that M-CQDs have a shift of the peak associated with C-O and C=O oxygen bonds due to doping metals into carbon quantum dots, and a band at 550 cm-1, which is related to M-O bonds. N-CQDs and M-CQDs exhibited impressive PL behavior as an antibacterial factor against representative Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacterial strains. According to the findings and comparisons of the average halo zone of the anti-bactericidal activity of the prepared samples and MIC methods, N-CQDs and M-CQDs demonstrate excellent prevention versus bacteria with the best MIC of 0.5 mg/L. Co-CQDs had the greatest inhibitory effect against S. aureus with an average halo zone diameter of 15 mm, and E. coli with an average halo zone diameter of 13 mm. This toxicity is due to the generation of reactive oxygen species and the interaction between the M-CQDs and cells.

Antibacterial activity and minimal inhibitory concentration of the quantum dot
Image, graphical abstract

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, CQDs have gained much attention due to their optical and electronic features [1], which have led to their use in various fields such as photocatalysis and biological processes [2,3]. This high potential of CQDs's applications is due to their spherical morphology, layered structure, and functional surface with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which causes their solubility and biocompatibility [4,5]. Compared to CQDs, M-CQDs show higher fluorescence intensity [6]. However, M-CQDs illustrate low toxicity and solubility in water, which accelerates their use in bio-imaging, bio-sensing, drug delivery, and anti-bacterial [4,7,8]. On the other hand, bacterial poisons are a significant concern in healthcare, especially with the enhancement of bacterial stability to antibiotics, demanding the progress of replacement antimicrobial strategies [1,2]. Moreover, antibacterial materials are important in packaging, mostly in the case of food packaging and healthcare [9].

Thus, developing cost-effective, efficient, and environmentally friendly nanomaterials with high photoactivity and minimal toxicity is essential. " Green conditions " refer to synthesis approaches that reduce the use of hazardous chemicals, lower energy requirements, and limit harmful by-products, thereby enhancing the sustainability of the process. This approach is vital for minimizing the environmental impact of nanomaterials and ensuring their safe use, particularly in biomedical applications [10]. Photo-activated CQDs are one of the most adequate semiconductors to constrain antibacterial growth, with the slightest side consequence for living beings [10]. The CQDs could play as a great antibacterial in the case of gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria due to their surface charges which provide electrostatic interaction. So, they are effective controllers for bacteria growth [11]. In addition, ultra-high quantum yield photoluminescence of the M-CQDs through reversible charge transfer improves their bacteria inhibitor behavior [12]. Travlou et al. investigated the influence of electrostatic interactions of Ag-loaded N and S-doped CQDs against B. subtilis and E. coli [13]. They displayed that amides and amines have an impressive role in the elimination of bacteria. N-doped CQDs lead to additional cell death owing to the interaction between the negative charge of the cell membrane and the positive charge of nitrogen functional groups which cause the production of ROS and cell death. Therefore, the combination of CQDs with metal ions by occupying some positions in the carbon dots improves some of their properties [14,15]. It seems that the easier transfer of electrons in the M-CQDs leads to their higher fluorescence quantum efficiency and thus is effective in improving their bacterial inhibition [16,17].

Besides, their solvothermal and facile synthesis through inexpensive and eco-friendly methods makes them more attractive. Utilizing natural and abundant sources, such as sucrose, glucose, citric acid, or even milk and fruit juice, for their green synthesis further enhances their sustainability and reduces the environmental footprint [18–20]. Herein, a series of M-CQDs was synthesized using citric acid as a precursor and combined with metal ions such as Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+ in aqueous acidic media under room temperature and characterized using several physicochemical methods. M-CQDs were derived as antibacterial in the case of gram-positive and gram-negative in comparison to N-CQDs by the generation of superoxide radicals. Unlike the other reports, we try to compare the antibacterial activity of metal-free N-CQDs with M-CQDs under the same conditions. Particularly, changes in the surface of N-CQDs by doping with metals, the bandgap energy, and electron-hole separation can considerably improve, so the anti-bacterial, inhibition zone, and sensing properties increase.
2-Hydroxypropane-1,2,3-tricarboxylic acid (C6H8FeO7 +2) and metal acetate (M(CH3CO2)2, M= Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn) were purchased from Merck.

Carbon Dots were prepared by using the hydrothermal method from C6H8FeO7 +2. Briefly, 1 g of carbon source (citric acid and ethylene diamine (0.5 mL)) was added into 20 mL pure liquid de-hydrogen mono oxide, and the mixture was sonicated for 5 min, then it was moved to an autoclave at 200 °C [6]. After 5 h heating, the colorless solution with pH: 5.5 was prepared, centrifuged at 7000 rpm, and dialyzed (5000 Da) for 24 h The N-CQDs were stored in dark conditions and low temperatures for future studies.

The doped carbon dots with metals were synthesized by adding some metal acetate to the carbon source solution. Thereby, 0.1 g iron (II) acetate dissolved in 10 mL deionized water. After adding it into the carbon source (citric acid and ethylene diamine (0.5 mL)) solution with sonicating for 10 minutes, then heated, centrifuged at 7000 rpm, and dialyzed. Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn-CQDs were prepared with the same conditions.

The obtained N-CQDs and their derivations were characterized by several physicochemical methods. To characterize the optical properties, EDS (Energy-Dispersive Spectroscopy), Photoluminescence (PL) spectra using a fluorescent spectrophotometer (Photoluminescence, Varian Cary Eclipse), and UV–Vis spectrum (Varian Cary 100) were employed. Their structure was confirmed by FT-IR (VERTEC 70, Bruker, Germany), TEM (Transmission Electron Microscopy, JEOL 2011, Japan), Confocal Microscopy and Raman Spectroscopy (Unodron, 532 nm, South Korea(, and XRD (X-ray diffraction).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial properties of synthetic quantum dots on the tested pathogenic bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized quantum dots were tested on Mueller-Hinton agar (MHA) surfaces in the case of Escherichia coli PTCC1329 (Gram-negative) and Staphylococcus aureus PTCC112 (Gram-positive), using the good diffusion method to estimate the zone of inhibition [21]. Following the preparation of a microbial suspension with turbidity corresponding to half McFarland standard (10⁸ CFU/mL), the suspension was cultured on Mueller-Hinton agar (MHA) plates using a sterile glass rod. After the plates were allowed to dry, four wells, each with a 5 mm diameter, were created at appropriate distances from one another using a sterile Pasteur pipette. Each well was filled with 50 µL of the quantum dot preparations (10 g/L). The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 hours. After incubation, the diameter of the inhibition zone was measured to assess bacterial sensitivity. Statistical analysis was performed using analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey's post-hoc test to determine the significance of the results. All analyses were conducted using GraphPad Prism version 10, with a significance threshold set at 95% (p < 0.05).

In the microplate method for determining the MIC, MHB was used along with different concentrations of quantum dots and broad-spectrum antibiotics, such as chloramphenicol. Each well received 10 µL of bacterial suspension (at a 0.5 McFarland turbidity), followed by 140 µL of MHB containing the specified concentrations of quantum dots (0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 g/L) and corresponding levels of chloramphenicol. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 hours. After 3 hours, 10 µL of a 20 g/L 2,3,5-triphenyl tetrazolium chloride solution was added to each well, and incubation continued for 3 hours. In wells where microbial growth occurred, viable bacteria reduced the triphenyl tetrazolium chloride to red formazan salts, resulting in a red color. After 3 hours, the plates were removed from the incubator and inspected for the appearance of red color. The MIC was determined as the lowest concentration with no microbial growth or red color formation observed.

In this study, we employed a simple and cost-effective hydrothermal approach to synthesize N-CQDs and M-CQDs, in line with the principles of green chemistry. Green chemistry advocates for techniques that minimize the use of toxic substances, reduce energy consumption, and decrease harmful by-products, thereby mitigating environmental impacts while ensuring safer applications, particularly in biomedical contexts. Several studies highlight that the use of natural carbon sources like citric acid, together with environmentally friendly metals, can produce nanoparticles with exceptional optical and biological properties [19,20,22–25]. Studies by Zhan et al. and Qasim et al. emphasize the advantages of renewable resources and mild synthesis conditions, aligning well with the hydrothermal method applied in this research [19,22]. Therefore, the eco-friendly synthesis of N-CQDs and M-CQDs presents a promising strategy for creating nanoparticles that exhibit both low toxicity and high stability.

Water-soluble M-CQDs were produced by a facile hydrothermal strategy. The combination of C₆H₈O₇ with metal ions is economical and rich in multifunctional groups (–COOH, –OH), supplying both carbon and metal sources for the formation of fluorescent N-CQDs and M-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the ability to absorb visible light by N-CQDs was greatly enhanced after doping, while N-CQDs absorb strongly in the UV region. M-CDs show a broadband absorption from λ = 200 nm to 800 nm. The UV absorption of samples demonstrated a well-observed band at 258 nm owing to the Π–Π* transitions. The UV spectrum of N-CQDs had 258 and 300 nm peaks, assigned to unfilled bond transitions of sp2 hybridization. The broad bands at 310 and 400 nm would relate to the n–Π* transition of the C=O and C=N from the structures. In the case of M-CQDs, the λmax= 540, 770, and 700 nm were obtained for Co-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, and Ni-CQDs, respectively. Meanwhile, the absorption intensity of Fe-CQDs and Zn-CQDs is higher than that of pure N-CQDs with wide shoulders at 350–400 nm. This increase in intensity absorption of M-CQDs could be attributed to the presence of M dopants, producing M-CQDs charge-transfer absorption [11]. The intensity of normalized PL of Ni-CQDs and Zn-CQDs is less than others, which causes an increasing rate of charge separation in these doped carbon quantum dots [26–28]. Fig. 1b indicates that Ni and Zn-CQDs unlike other CQDs samples have broad emission peaks between 390–500 nm related to deep levels, surface defects, and band edge. While other QDs indicate a sharp emission assigned to the movement of an electron from surface states or conduction bands to the electronic levels of M [29].

The PL spectra of N-CQDs, Co-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, Fe-CQDs, Ni-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs were analyzed to understand their PL properties (Fig. 2 ). The photoluminescence of the materials demonstrates a multi-phonon procedure related to the relaxation that occurs by the numerous electronic levels within the samples’ forbidden band gap [30]. A close approach to the PL trend revealed smooth changes from cobalt-dopped CQD toward zinc CQD due to losing the Centro-symmetry ligand field from Oh to Td, respectively. Also, upon symmetry reduction, PL intensity should be decreased.

The functional groups on the surface of these particles were studied by FT-IR (Fig. 3 a) and were defined by oxygen-containing groups (OH, C=O, COO), with the bands at 3410, 1720, and 1400∼1000 cm-1, respectively [31]. The peaks at 1669 and 2826–2936 cm−1 related to the C = C and C–H vibration bond, respectively. In the FT-IR spectrum of M-CQDs, a band at 570–590 cm−1 should be assigned to M–O, which indicates the successful formation of M-CQDs [32]. The FT-IR results showed the availability of hydrophilic groups on the surface of N-CQDs and M-CQDs validating their excellent water solubility and stability in aqueous systems [11,33].

CQD and M-CQDs demonstrate a broad pattern with an amorphous nature under 25.00° related to the (002) plane of citric acid carbonization to generate the N-CQDs during the hydrothermal process [34]. The presence of metals in the N-CQDs lattice illustrates a shift in the (002) to higher 2 theta, which is related to a reduction in the stacking of a graphite-like sheet [35]. Nevertheless, no significant diffraction peaks of metals were observed in the M-CQDs due to their small amount and equal dispersion of them (Fig. 3b). There is no more peak in the XRD pattern of M-CQDs displaying purity phase generation.

The TEM imaging of M-CQDs illustrates that they are uniform in size and shape, with no confirmation of aggregation (Fig. 4 a). The M-CQDs's morphology was indicated using HRTEM images (different scales, Fig. 4). The regularly dispersed spherical dark spots are N-CQDs without any packing defects.

Fig. 5 demonstrates the EDS analysis of N-CQDs and M-CQDs samples. In EDS analysis related to each M-CQD, the presence of M in the structure of N-CQDs is confirmed. Accordingly, the atomic percentage of M:C:O in N-CQDs, Co-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, Fe-CQDs, Ni-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs was 0:57.63:42.37, 0.89:54.75:44.36, 0.60:51.94:47.46, 0.87:56.08:43.05, 0.71:52.39:46.89, and 0.92:54.88:44.20, respectively.

The Raman spectrum of N-CQDs and M-CQDs indicate two strong peaks at 1306 and 1472 cm−1 for N-CQDs and at 1162 and 1405 cm−1, which are related to the D and G bands, respectively (Fig. 6 ). In the case of N-GQDs, the ID/IG ratio is 0.63, while for M-CQDs is 0.56. The higher intensity of the G band than the D band illustrates that the composition of graphite is more than amorphous carbon. Therefore, it could prove the successful production of N-CQDs and M-CQDs.

The results in Fig. 7 indicate that carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) doped with Co exhibiting the highest antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus. Specifically, Co-CQDs show the largest inhibition zones, with an average diameter of 15 mm for S. aureus and 13 mm for E. coli. In contrast, Ni-CQDs demonstrate the lowest inhibitory effect, with inhibition zone diameters averaging 6 mm for S. aureus and 5 mm for E. coli. Statistical analysis of the data also shows that the differences in inhibition for Co-CQDs compared to other dopants are highly significant (p < 0.0001), indicating a remarkable efficacy of this compound relative to other CQDs. In contrast, Ni-CQDs do not show significant differences in inhibition when compared to other compounds and rank at the lowest level. These findings suggest that Co doping enhances the antibacterial properties of carbon quantum dots, making it a potentially effective option against infections caused by Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, while other dopants, such as Ni-CQDs, exhibit lower efficacy and may require further optimization.

The MIC results in Fig. 8 indicate that N-CQDs and Fe-doped CQDs demonstrate the highest antibacterial susceptibility, with a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 0.5 gs per liter. This suggests a strong antibacterial effect at lower concentrations. In contrast, Ni-doped CQDs and other formulations, including Zn-CQDs and Cu-CQDs, show a lower antibacterial effect with MIC values greater than 1 gram per liter, indicating that higher concentrations are needed to inhibit bacterial growth. The comparison with chloramphenicol, a broad-spectrum antibiotic, highlights that the quantum dot formulations possess notable antibacterial activity, as shown by the observable color changes in the wells, which correspond to bacterial inhibition. These results suggest that specific dopants, such as Fe and undoped N-CQDs, enhance the antibacterial properties of N-CQDs, making them more effective against the pathogens tested.

Carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and metal-doped CQDs (M-CQDs) were synthesized using the hydrothermal method, in line with the principles of green chemistry. The obtained samples were characterized by UV–Vis, EDS, FT-IR, XRD, and PL techniques. The bactericidal activity of the samples was assessed through inhibition zone measurements. The results demonstrate that cobalt (Co) doping significantly enhanced the antibacterial properties of the N-CQDs, particularly against Gram-negative bacteria. Additionally, the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values revealed that both N-CQDs and Fe-CQDs exhibited the highest antibacterial susceptibility, with an MIC of 0.5 g/L. Scheme 1

Payam Ansari: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mahdieh Ghobadifard: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sajjad Mohebbi: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Morahem Ashengroph: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors like to acknowledge the University of Kurdistan, Ayatollah Boroujerdi University, and the Labs Nets for their support in the characterization of materials.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic conversion of MB dye using Co3O4 QDs-Ag2MoO4 as an active heterojunction photocatalyst under visible light irradiation
